coredata.dc:title: An in-situ blocking strategy for improved anti-interference inspection of AFB1 based on hollow covalent organic framework capsules with commodious and undisturbed microenvironment
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lu
Wang, Jing
Li, Chun-Yang
Wang, Man-Man
Liu, Jing-Min
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: This work proposed an in-situ blocking strategy for improved anti-interference and signal-amplified inspection of hazards via constructing hollow covalent organic framework (HCOF) capsules. An aptamer-FRET nanoprobe integrated with carbon dots and CuS was introduced into the micro-capsule as signal indicator to demonstrate the proof-of-concept. The HCOF was successfully prepared by removing the metal–organic frameworks (MOF) core from the MOF@COF that had been preloaded with the nanoprobes under mild conditions. Meanwhile, the hydrophobic surface of HCOF enhanced the adsorption and penetration of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) into the capsule to interact with the nanoprobes. This strategy was applied to detect AFB1 in food samples, achieving a linear response of 1–300 nM along with a detection limit of 0.3 nM. Selectivity test verified that the prepared sensing platform could specifically recognize AFB1 without complex sample pretreatment. This study provides new ideas for improved anti-interference inspection of hazards against complex sample matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxin B1
Aptasensor
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Carbon dots
Hollow covalent organic frameworks

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272398 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of CDs-Aptamer@CuS@HCOF composite fluorescent probe for AFB1 detection.

(A) Illustration of detection procedure by FRET-based nanobiosensor; (B) The fluorescence spectrum of CDs-Apt and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CuS; (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs and CDs-Apt; (D) Effect of AFB1 on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS probe. The inset showed the photographs of solution state of the CDs-Apt (a), CDs-Apt@CuS + AFB1 (b) and the CDs-Apt@CuS (c). The concentration of AFB1 was 10 nM in the above experiment. (E) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-Apt in the presence of different concentrations of CuS (0, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 μg mL−1). Inset: The fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs-Apt at different concentrations of CuS (from 0 to 60 μg mL−1).Where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt in the absence and presence of CuS, respectively.

(A) TEM image of the CDs (a-b), CuS (c), aptasensor@ZIF-90 (d), aptasensor@HCOF (e-f); (B) The average diameter statistical calculation of aptasensor@ZIF-90 (a), aptasensor@HCOF (b); (C) EDS mapping images of the aptasensor@ZIF-90; (D) EDS mapping images of the aptasensor@ZIF-90@COF.

The characteristic of ZIF-90, COF, ZIF-90@COF and HCOF: FT-IR spectrum (A), XRD spectra (B), BET analysis (C). (D) Effect of the embedded nanomaterials on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS probe.

(A) The quenching effect of CuS on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS in different incubation time. Inset: The binding time effect of CDs-Apt and AFB1 on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Apt@CuS probe; (B) Effect of temperature on the assay performance; (C) Effect of NaCl concentration on the assay performance; (D) Effect of pH value on the assay performance. The concentration of AFB1 was 10 nM in the above experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs-Apt@CuS in the presence of different concentrations of AFB1 (5–200 nM), the inset shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 of CDs-Apt@CuS and AFB1 concentration; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the aptasensor@HCOF in the presence of different concentrations of AFB1 (1–300 nM), the inset shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 of aptasensor@HCOF and AFB1 concentration; (C) The relatively fluorescence intensity of aptasensor@HCOF probe after incubation with AFB1 and different interfering substances; (D) The relatively fluorescence intensity of aptasensor probe after incubation with AFB1 and different interfering substances.

head.ce:title: An in-situ blocking strategy for improved anti-interference inspection of AFB1 based on hollow covalent organic framework capsules with commodious and undisturbed microenvironment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, North China University of Science and Technology, No.21 Bohai Road, Caofeidian, Tangshan 063210, Hebei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: North China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, North China University of Science and Technology, No.21 Bohai Road, Caofeidian, Tangshan 063210, Hebei, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of CDs-Aptamer@CuS@HCOF composite fluorescent probe for highly sensitive enrichment and determination of AFB1 detection from corns.

This work proposed an in-situ blocking strategy for improved anti-interference and signal-amplified inspection of hazards via constructing hollow covalent organic framework (HCOF) capsules. An aptamer-FRET nanoprobe integrated with carbon dots and CuS was introduced into the micro-capsule as signal indicator to demonstrate the proof-of-concept. The HCOF was successfully prepared by removing the metal–organic frameworks (MOF) core from the MOF@COF that had been preloaded with the nanoprobes under mild conditions. Meanwhile, the hydrophobic surface of HCOF enhanced the adsorption and penetration of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) into the capsule to interact with the nanoprobes. This strategy was applied to detect AFB1 in food samples, achieving a linear response of 1–300 nM along with a detection limit of 0.3 nM. Selectivity test verified that the prepared sensing platform could specifically recognize AFB1 without complex sample pretreatment. This study provides new ideas for improved anti-interference inspection of hazards against complex sample matrix.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, food safety has gained widespread attention since safety is the minimum requirement for food consumption. Meanwhile, food safety is a tough nut, especially for the highly poisonous food hazards present in complicated matrix at extremely low levels. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), classified as a group I human carcinogen, hold significant risk of liver and cancer toxicity (Zhang, Li, Yang, Zhang, Zhang, Xiao, et al., 2011). With the growing toxicological evidence for AFB1, many countries have enacted relevant laws to regulate AFB1 in foods to ensure they are not harmful to human health. Singapore and Japan set the regulatory limits at 5 mg/kg and 10 mg/kg, respectively (Zhang, Lu, Yang, Guo, & Nie, 2019). Because the current agricultural production mode and food processing technology can not completely remove AFB1, the food sample is very susceptible to AFB1 contamination. Besides, most of the existing detection method of AFB1 is time-consuming with complex sample preparation steps. Therefore, the development of fast and feasible inspection methods for AFB1 in food is of vital importance for food safety monitoring and body health protection (see Fig. 1 ).

The high complexity of food matrices, especially containing large amounts of starch and fat, sets considerable challenge for food hazard extraction and enrichment. Especially for AFB1 present in complicated food samples with extremely low levels, effort-consuming sample pretreatment operations are inevitable, which is wary to cause the loss of method accuracy or feasibility. Therefore, the major challenge is to find a way that could overcome the matrix interference while ensure the rapidness and sensitivity of the detection method. The development of framework materials inspired us to use a hollow protection layer strategy to solve the above problems. As a new field of porous material research, covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) gained great attention in pre-treatment of food samples (Li, Zhang, Cai, Lei, Altoe, Hong, et al., 2018; Sun, Aguila, Perman, Earl, Abney, Cheng, et al., 2017). MOF has become one of the current hot spots in the field of adsorbent materials because of its specific surface area, adjustable pore size, and easy functional modification (Wang, Rehman, kong, et al., 2021; Guo, Liu, Kong, et al., 2019). Evidence suggested that the structure of MOF shell, embedded with biomacromolecule, could be digested to free biomacromolecules as well as keeping their bioactivities. Therefore, MOFs is a suitable sacrificial template for the construction of COF smart capsules (Liang, Ricco, Doherty, Styles, Bell, Kirby, et al., 2015). Uniting the advantages of MOFs and COFs to form a “smart capsule” may improve the effect of biomacromolecules (Li, Karimi, Gong, Dai, Safarifard, & Jiang, 2021; Yuan, Tan, Wang, Wang, Wang, Yan, et al., 2021). For example, the MOF shell can prevent detection unity or signal indicator from complex synthetic conditions, while the high stability of COF can allow the break of MOF, resulting in an active and unhindered nanosensor in the protective HCOF capsule. ZIF-90 can load various bioactive macromolecules and protect them from harsh conditions while also being feasible for digesting under mild acidic conditions. Therefore, it is practical to construct the hollow HCOF by removal of ZIF-90 from ZIF-90@COF to give a large inner cavity so that the signal indicator inside could work better within a protective HCOF capsule.

The introduction of HCOF protective capsule would provide a commodious and undisturbed microenvironment inside for AFB1 inspection, favoring the improved anti-interference strategy. Meanwhile, the combination of signal indicator inside the HCOF capsule could accomplish the AFB1 detection method. At present, traditional inspection methods including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Nguyen, Tozlovanu, Tran, & Pfohlleszkowicz, 2007) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Armorini, Altafini, Zaghini, & Roncada, 2015) still need confront expensive procedures, complex sample preparation, and the inability to perform in-situ screening. Development of optical sensing via luminescent nanoprobes always demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity as well as rapidness and time-saving, thus have attracted great research interest of food-safety inspection field (Nguyen, Vu, Pham, Tran, Le Thi, & Vo Thi, 2020).

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system is widely used in the fields of biosensing and biomedical imaging because of its simplicity, rapidity, sensitivity and specificity (Yang, Zhang, & Yan, 2018). It refers to an energy transfer phenomenon that occurs between two close fluorescent molecules (Choi, Ha, Shin, Lee, & Choi, 2021; Kaur, Ellison, & Dhakal, 2021; Yi & Zhang, 2021). In the FRET system, it is important to select the appropriate energy donor and acceptor. As a novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) with a diameter of <10 nm have spherical structures and excellent physicochemical properties (Ali, Ghosh, & Jana, 2020). Moreover, as it can be easily prepared using abundant and inexpensive materials, it is widely applied as electronic components, biomarkers, nanoprobes and analytical assays (Atabaev, 2018; Feng & Qian, 2018). To broaden the application of CDs in hazard determination and improve sensitivity and selectivity, attempt have been made by modifying CDs surface with some affinity ligands, such as biomacromolecules, antibodies and various organic small molecules (Zhou, Feng, Zhou, Fang, Ma, Liu, et al., 2015). In terms of molecule detection, the application of aptamers as affinity ligands to modify CDs was a promising candidate for specific recognition and binding of targets (Vasilescu & Marty, 2016). Aptamers are relatively easy to be obtained and maintained, which could recognize various target molecules with high affinity and specificity. Because of the predominant fluorescence quenching efficiency, CuS has attracted abundant attention in the material sciences (Jiang, Zhang, Wu, Zhai, Li, Luan, et al., 2018; Zhang, Fan, Yang, Yang, & Wong, 2021). Owing to the electrostatic attraction between aptamer and CuS, CDs-Apt was adsorbed on the surface of CuS, resulting to the quench effect on CDs-Apt fluorescence based on the FRET mechanism. After addition of AFB1, CDs-Apt/AFB1 was formed, getting distance from CuS and leading to fluorescence recovery. Therefore, the developed CD-Apt@CuS nanosensor was expected to offer a significative way for rapid sensing of AFB1, and suitable for signal indicator inside the HCOF capsule.

Herein, we proposed a new in-situ blocking strategy for improved anti-interference and signal-amplified inspection of hazards via constructing hollow covalent organic framework capsules that enabled sensitive inspection of food hazards at extremely low levels in complex food matrix. HCOF framework is responsible to block the entry of macromolecules in food samples and gave commodious and undisturbed microenvironment for the FRET aptasensor (CDs-Apt@CuS) to AFB1. The optical nanoprobes were composed of CDs-Apt and CuS for specific recognition of AFB1. Besides, the presence of unreacted aldehyde groups in the ligand 2-imidazolecarbaldehyde (ICA) made COF successfully in-situ growth on the surface of ZIF-90. Then, we constructed the HCOF hollow capsules by removing ZIF-90 from the composite ZIF-90@COF to protect the aptasensor from macromolecular interference. Meanwhile, the hydrophobic surface of HCOF could enhance the adsorption and penetration of AFB1 into the capsule, which would also contribute to the interaction of AFB1 with the nanoprobes. Selectivity test verified that the HCOF capsules embedded with luminescent nanoprobes could be an effective platform for specific recognition of AFB1 in practical applications. This work provides new ideas for improved anti-interference and in situ inspection of hazards against complex sample matrix.

p-Phenylenediamine (PPDA), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), 2-imidazolecarbaldehyde (ICA, >98.0%), copper(II) chloride (CuCl2), sodium citrate, 1,3,5-Tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene (TPB, >97.0%), lead chloride (PbCl2, >99.99%), 1,4-Phthalaldehyde (TPA, >98.0%), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, >99.0%), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 40,000), and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, >99.0%) were bought from Sigma Aldrich. HPLC-grade AFB1, AFM1, AFB2, ST, AFG1, AFG2, ZEN, DON and OTA were Sigma (Shanghai, China). The 50-mer 3′-carboxy-modified AFB1 aptamer oligonucleotide (5′-GTTGGGCACGTGTTGTCTCTCTGTGTCTCGTGCCCTTCGCTAGGCCCACA-3′) was synthesized by Shanghai Generay Biotech Co (Shanghai, China).

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were taken on JEM-2800. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured on the D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a TENSOR 37 spectrometer. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were taken on a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 automatic volumetric instrument through which the samples were degassed at 100 ℃ for 8 h under vacuum. Moreover, the Shimadzu UV-29100 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was used for the UV–vis absorption spectra.

A total of 0.1 g of p-phenylenediamine was weighted into 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol, then sonicated until completely dissolved. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave and reacted in an oven at 180 ℃ for 10 h. The obtained was cooled to room temperature and the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder. Meanwhile, carboxy-modified AFB1 aptamers were dissolved in TE buffer (1X). The activation of carboxy-modified AFB1 aptamers was conducted using EDC (1 mM) and NHS (1 mM) for 30 min. The CDs were incubated with carboxy-modified AFB1 aptamers for 2 h.

Typically, the traditional synthesis of CuS was as follows. CuCl2 (0.13 g, 1 mmol), sodium citrate (0.20 g, 0.68 mmol) and 1 mL of sodium sulfide solution (Na2S, 1 M) were added to 1000 mL of an aqueous solution, then stirred 5 min. The mixture was heated to 90 ℃ and stirred for 15 min. Finally, purification of CDs-Apt was conducted by dialysis. The CuS was added to aptamer conjugated CDs solution followed by a 30 min mild shaking, which obtained CDs-Apt@CuS.

For the synthesis of CDs-Apt@CuS@ZIF-90, 10.00 mL of CDs-Apt@CuS solution was added to DI water (25.00 mL), in which ICA (960.00 mg), PVP (100.00 mg) was dissolved. Zinc acetate dihydrate solution was also prepared in DI water (742.60 mg, 25.00 mL). The above two solutions were mixed and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. Finally, the synthesized CDs-Apt@CuS@ZIF-90 was washed with excess D.I. water.

Typically, TPB (0.08 mmol) was dispersed into 25.00 mL of methanol and then sonicated until completely dissolved. The CDs-Apt@CuS@ZIF-90 were added to the above solution and stirred for another 1 h. Then, followed by the addition of TPA (0.12 mmol) and 100.00 μL PbCl2, shaken for 1 min. The above system was kept still at room temperature for 24 h. The obtained yellow precipitates were centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and washed three times with ethanol. Eventually, the HCOF was developed by removing the ZIF-90 from ZIF-90@COF. This work was found that the ZIF-90 could be destroyed by citric acid phosphate buffer (pH = 5.0). To synthesis CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF, about 20.00 mg of CDs-Apt@CuS@ZIF-90@COF were etched in 10.00 mL citric acid phosphate buffer (pH = 5.0) for 1 min. The obtained yellow precipitates were centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and washed several times with ethanol.

50 μL of 100 nM CDs-Apt@CuS or CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF and 50 μL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0, 200 mM NaCl) were placed in a series of colorimeter tubes. After that, different concentrations of AFB1 solutions were added into this mixture for 20 min. Then, 5.0 mL distilled water was used to dilute the solution. The fluorescence spectra were measured from 530 nm to 700 nm after 20 min reaction. The excitation wavelength was 590 nm.

In this research, we presented a micro-capsule protection strategy coupled with a rapid nanosensing platform that enabled sensitive inspection of food hazards at extremely low levels in complex food matrices. To be specific, a hollow covalent organic framework (HCOF) micro-capsule was constructed as a protective shell to encapsulate the signal indicator, which would block the interference of macromolecules from the food matrix. The classical FRET nanosensor was introduced as the signal indicator for quantification of target. As for the FRET nanoprobe for specific recognition of AFB1, we chose CDs and CuS as energy donor and acceptor, respectively. Fig. 2 A depicted the principle mechanism of the proposed method. To demonstrate the feasibility of the combination of CDs-Apt and CuS, we measured the overlap between the fluorescence emission of CDs and the absorption spectrum of CuS. As shown in Fig. 2B, CDs exhibited a distinct emission peak at around 590 nm, which partially overlapped with the absorption spectrum of CuS. As a consequence, the fluorescence of the CDs could be essentially burst in close proximity to CuS when there was no available target. To explore the mechanism of CDs-Apt connected with CuS, the zeta potential of aptamer-CDs and CuS have been measured, which indicated the negatively charged CuS (−13.3 ± 0.4 mV) would adsorb the weak positively charged aptamer-CDs (+2.7 ± 0.2 mV). Upon addition of target, the aptamer would be conjugated with target, occupying the exposed bases and breaking the interaction of aptamer-CDs with CuS. The developed CDs were verified to have amino structures by FT-IR experiments (Fig. S1). Based on the CDs structure, the carboxyl modified AFB1 aptamer was designed. We observed a typical DNA absorption peak at 260 nm in CDs-Apt, which verified that the AFB1 aptamer had been successfully pre-covalently linked to the carbon dots (Fig. 2C). Meanwhile, a clear emission peak at of CDs-Apt around 590 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt did not change significantly compared with that of the original CDs. As shown in Fig. 2E, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt gradually decreased with the increase of CuS concentration in the presence of CuS, and stabilized as the CuS concentration reached 60 μg/mL. The above results clearly indicated that CDs-Apt was adsorbed on the CuS surface and the CDs-Apt@CuS composite was formed through the FRET mechanism between the two energy groups. In order to demonstrate the feasibility of the developed luminescent nanoprobe for AFB1 detection, the AFB1 standard solution was added to the CDs-Apt@CuS complex solution, and the CDs-Apt tended to bind to the target, forming an aptamer-target composite that kept the aptamer at a certain distance from CuS. In comparison with the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs-Apt@CuS probe, the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs-Apt@CuS-AFB1 system increased by approximately 4 times (Fig. 2D). The inset in Fig. 2D could clearly show the fluorescence changes from the CDs-Apt (a), CDs-Apt@CuS + AFB1 (b) to the CDs-Apt@CuS (c). The above results further demonstrated the inhibitory effect of AFB1 on the interaction between CDs-Apt and CuS. Additionally, the photostability of CDs-Apt in different solutions were been measured, of which F and F0 in the vertical coordinates referred to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS at different time and the initial value (Fig. S2). The results showed that the CDs-Apt@CuS is stable in water, PBS and citric acid PB in 600 min.

Complex food samples contain large amounts of starch and fat, which undoubtedly increases the difficulty of sample extraction and enrichment. It is crucial to find a nanomaterial that blocks the entry of macromolecules in food samples without compromising the detection efficiency of the aptamer sensor for AFB1 is crucial. Several reports had demonstrated that the structure of MOF shell, embedded with biomacromolecule, could be digested to free biomacromolecules as well as keeping their bioactivities. Therefore, MOFs is a suitable sacrificial template for the construction of COF smart capsules (Liang et al., 2015). The MOF shell can prevent aptamer from complex synthetic conditions, while the high stability of COF can allow the break of MOF, resulting in an active and unhindered aptasensor in the protective HCOF capsule. The presence of unreacted aldehyde groups, which can participate in the amine–aldehyde condensation reaction to develop COF, ZIF-90 was a suitable candidate to save as a template in the construction of COF shell. Evidence suggested that the structure of ZIF-90 could be etched in aqueous solutions with pH < 7.0. In order to maintain the activity of the aptasensor during the synthesis process and its stability during the ZIF-90 etch, an acid-stable COF need to be developed to construct capsules. In this regard, COF-TPB-TPA was selected (Ma, Zheng, He, Li, Guo, Lin, et al., 2019; Uribe-Romo, Doonan, Furukawa, Oisaki, & Yaghi, 2011). Compared with the traditional acetic acid catalysis in the Schiff base reaction, the PbCl2 was chosen as a catalyst to prevent the interference of aptasensor@ZIF-90 by the acetic acid. Based on the above view, a core–shell nanomaterial ZIF-90@COF was developed to prevent macromolecular interference in this work.

Meanwhile, removing ZIF-90 from ZIF-90@COF could form a hollow structure, which provided a large inner cavity so that the aptasensor could work better without interference. The morphology and size of CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 A (a-b), the prepared CDs were well dispersed with a spherical structure, and the average particle size was 3 nm. TEM images clearly showed that the CuS and CuS@ZIF-90 were uniform with average sizes of 8 nm and 505 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A (c-d)). The TEM image of CDs-Apt@CuS was provided in the Fig. S3, the connection between CuS and CDs-Apt could not be seen only from TEM images. Further, the presence of C, N, Zn and Cu in Energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS) element mapping (Fig. 3C) also illustrated the aptasensor embedded with ZIF-90 successfully. Fig. 3D showed the aptasensor@ZIF-90@COF structure, which proved that as the pristine COF can in-situ growth on the ZIF-90 surface, forming “core–shell” structural. In order to preferably exhibit the “core–shell” structural, the single EDS mapping included Zn and N was showed in Fig. S4. The Zn referred to the ZIF-90 core while the N represented the whole composite material. Several researches have demonstrated that ZIF-90 could be digested in aqueous solution with pH < 7.0. After citric acid phosphate buffer (pH = 5.0) treatment, the hollow structure formed (Fig. 3A (e-f)). In order to sure complete etching, etching time was of vital importance. Therefore, we optimized the etching conditions by using 10.00 mL citric acid phosphate buffer (pH = 5.0) for different times. By measuring the concentration of Zn2+ in the supernatant after etch CDs-Apt@CuS@ZIF-90@COF, the Table S1 showed the concentration of Zn2+ reached balance after 1 min. So 1 min was chosen as suitable etching time in the fellow experiments. Especially, the disappearance of Zn EDS element mapping (Fig. S5) further illustrated the ZIF-90 was etched successfully. At the same time, the elements percent of the CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF have been measured and listed the table to present the results. From the Table S2 we could further verify the successful synthesis of CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF. As shown in Fig. 3B, about 200 nanoparticles were stochastically selected for measure through obtained TEM images. The results showed the average diameter of aptasensor@ZIF-90 and aptasensor@HCOF as 504.2 ± 11.0 nm and 618.8 ± 10.7 nm, respectively.

To demonstrate the successful synthesis of core–shell and hollow structure, the FT-IR and XRD measurements were performed on a series of nanomaterials. FT-IR demonstrated the successful construction of ZIF-90@COF and the subsequent HCOF (Fig. 4 A). The main stretching vibration peaks of CO and NH of ZIF-90 were appeared at 1670 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1. Besides, the peaks at around 1560 and 1620 cm−1 were attributed to the CN typical vibrations, which were in conformity with formerly reported by previous work (Lv, Liu, Yang, Li & Wang, 2021), demonstrating COF succeed in growing on the ZIF-90 surface. After citric acid phosphate buffer (pH = 5.0) treatment, the respectively peaks of ZIF-90 disappeared. Moreover, the crystallization of HCOF was measured by XRD (Fig. 4B). The characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-90 at 2θ = 7.3°, 10.3°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.4°, 18° correspond to the (011), (200), (112), (022), (013) and (222) facets could be clearly seen. The diffraction peaks of the COF was well in consistent with the previous report (Lv, Liu, Yang, Li, & Wang, 2021). Besides, all the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-90 and COF presented to the XRD pattern of ZIF-90@COF, while the diffraction pattern of ZIF-90 was disappeared in XRD pattern of HCOF. Based on the above results, the core–shell structure of ZIF-90@COF was successfully obtained, and ZIF-90 could be entirely destroyed by citric acid phosphate buffer (pH = 5.0), forming a hollow structure of COF. From Fig. 4C, the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area was measured to be 773.1 m2/g. Similarly, the BET surface area of aptasensor@HCOF was 721.46 m2/g, which was close to that of HCOF.

Finally, we measured the applicability of HCOF capsules embedded with luminescent nanoprobes. Results showed that the aptasensor@ZIF-90 and aptasensor@ZIF-90@COF had little effect on the fluorescence of the aptasensor (Fig. 4D). We suspected that the core–shell ZIF-90@COF may block the connection between the aptasensor and the target. Fortunately, by removing ZIF-90 from aptasensor@ZIF-90@COF, the fluorescence of aptasensor@HCOF was greatly enhanced. The above results demonstrated that the formation of the hollow structure provided enough space for the aptasensor to recognize the target molecules. Moreover, we also measured the fluorescence recovery of the aptasensor@HCOF in the presence of AFB1. Fig. S6 showed that the aptasensor could recognize AFB1 more sensitively within a protective HCOF capsule.

To ensure that the probe functioned at optical performance, we conducted a series of optimization experiments. Firstly, we measured the reaction time between CDs-Apt and CuS. The fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 (where F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt at 590 nm in the presence and absence of CuS) dropped little by little as the incubation time increased. After CDs-Apt was incubated with CuS for 20 min, equilibrium was reached. As a result, the incubation time between CDs-Apt and CuS was chosen as 20 min and the concentration of AFB1 was 10 nM in the following optimization. Meanwhile, the interaction time between AFB1 and CDs-Apt has also been tested o enable the complete recovery of CDs-Apt. Fig. 5 A (inset) showed the F/F0 (F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS in the presence of AFB1 and CDs-Apt) gradually increased over incubation time and stabilized at about 20 min. Likewise, 20 min was considered as the appropriate incubation time for AFB1 and CDs-Apt@CuS.

From Fig. 5B, room temperature (25 °C) was the optimal temperature for the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CuS toward CDs-Apt combination as well as the specific binding efficiency of AFB1 and CDs-Apt. The formation of composites composed of CuS and CDs-Apt and CDs-Apt@CuS + AFB1 was adversely affected by either increasing or decreasing temperature. Meanwhile, 25 °C facilitated the synthesis of nanoprobes.

The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CuS toward CDs-Apt and CDs-Apt@CuS + AFB1 system was considered by adding different concentrations of NaCl solution. Fig. 5C showed the ionic strength gave an inconspicuous effect on the above-mentioned system. Hence, we chose a lower ionic strength (20 mM) as the fellow experiment condition.

Finally, it is known that pH could influence the stability of the aptamer. Considering that pH may also affect the stability of the composition of AFB1 and CDs-Apt@CuS, we measured the change in fluorescence intensity from pH 5.5 to 8.5. As seen in Fig. 5D, the pH value had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS fluorescence probes with and without AFB1. The efficiency of fluorescence quenching of CDs-Apt by CuS was relatively higher when the pH of the solution was 7.0 (F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt in the presence and absence of CuS). Meanwhile, the F/F0 (F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS in the presence of AFB1 and CDs-Apt) of CDs-Apt@CuS probe with AFB1 was maximum. Additionally, the pH may influence the performance of HCOF, so the change in fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF from pH 5.5 to 8.5 in the presence of AFB1 has been measured. As seen in Fig. S7, the pH value had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF fluorescence probes with AFB1. Combined with the above results, pH = 7.0 was selected as the optimal pH value.

To evaluate the stability of the probes in different batches, we measured the intensity of ten batches of the probes. The results showed the intensity of different batches was consistent (Fig. S8). Especially, in the presence of AFB1, the intensity of the probes was similar, which could verify the stability of the probes we developed. Besides, considering the stable structure and property of HCOF were crucial for accurate identification and determination performance, the particle size and surface area were monitored to evaluate the feasibility of COF and HCOF. By soaking COF and HCOF in different pH and common organic solvents, including PBS, HCl (0.1 M), and NaOH (0.1 M), acetonitrile and methanol for 48 h, the surface area (Fig. S9 A-B) and particle size (Fig. S9 C-D) of the materials showed insignificant changes, indicating the good stability of the COF and HCOF structure.

As mentioned before, the high fluorescence of CDs-Apt was attenuated by CuS in the absence of target AFB1. On the other side, the CDs-Apt/AFB1 complexes could be formed upon the addition of AFB1, which lead to CDs-Apt away from the CuS. Therefore, with higher concentrations of AFB1, higher fluorescence intensity was obtained. To ensure that the proposed HCOF embedded with fluorescence aptasensor was applied to detect the AFB1, we measured the fluorescence responses of the CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF sensor induced by a series of standard AFB1 concentrations. From Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF nanoprobe gradually increased with the increase of AFB1 concentration. Under the optimized conditions, there was a good linear relationship between the F/F0 of the single CDs-Apt@CuS fluorescence probe and AFB1 concentration in the range from 5 to 200 nM in Fig. 6A (inset). However, there was a good linear relationship between the F/F0 of the CDs-Apt@CuS within a protective HCOF capsule and AFB1 concentration in the range from 1 to 300 nM in Fig. 6B (inset). In contrast, the CDs-Apt@CuS in the protective HCOF recognized the AFB1 at a lower concentration, probably because of the ability of the single HCOF to adsorb AFB1. Meanwhile, as shown in Figs. S10A and S10B, the hydrophobic surface of HCOF has been enhanced compared with that of COF, which may also enhance the adsorption and penetration of AFB1 into the capsule, which would also contribute to the interaction of AFB1 with the nanoprobes. The detection limit for AFB1 was estimated to be 0.3 nM based on S/N = 3.

Table S3 provided a comparison of sensitivity and rapidity in this work with several existing sensors (Sabet, Hosseini, Khabbaz, Dadmehr, & Ganjali, 2017; Sergeyeva, Yarynka, Piletska, Lynnik, Zaporozhets, Brovko, et al., 2017; Wang, Zhao, Li, Liu, Cao, & Wei, 2018; Zhang et al., 2016; Yang, Zhang, & Yan, 2018; Goud, Hayat, Catanante, et al., 2017; Wu, Zhu, Li, et al., 2018). By contrast, the sensitivity of the developed fluorescence probes in this work was also comparable to those of other sensors except for electrochemical immunosensor based methods which require longer identification time and complicated experimental steps. Thus, this method based on FRET was convenient and sensitive to detect AFB1. Especially, the CDs-Apt@CuS within a protective HCOF capsule enhanced the sensitive of the fluorescent probe.

To assess the selectivity of the prepared CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF aptasensor for AFB1, some other mycotoxins, including AFB2, AFG1, AFG2, AFM1, DON, ST, ZEN, and OTA were tested. Fig. 6C and D showed the fluorescence intensity had no significant discrepancy when using either CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF or CDs-Apt@CuS, respectively, for sensing AFB1. At the same time, we further measured the selectivity of the probes for different ratios of the toxin mixture (Mixture 1 and Mixture 2 referred to the ratio of AFB1: other toxins as 1:10, respectively). Moreover, in Fig. 6C, when the HCOF protected material existed, the response of aptasensor@HCOF to AFG1 decreased while that of AFB2 and AFG2 increased. Such phenomenon was possibly because the AFG1 encountered more hindering by HCOF than that of AFB2 and AFG2, which would enter more into the HCOF capsule and induce higher response. Although the response of aptasensor@HCOF to AFB2 and AFG2 increased higher than of aptasensor, the existence of the HCOF, serving as protective capsule, is necessary when the interference substances and AFB1 are present simultaneously. HCOF could enhance the sensitivity of aptasenor for AFB1 in the interfering matrix. The above results illustrated that the constructed CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF probe showed almost no response to the interfering toxins, despite their concentration being much higher than AFB1. This phenomenon may be due to the weak ability of the aptamer to bind to interfering toxins. On the other hand, to emphasize the necessity of the presence of HCOF, we added the protein commonly found in the sample matrix, such as casein and β-lactoglobulin, as the AFB1 interfering substance to the CDs-Apt@CuS and CDs-Apt@CuSHCOF. As we speculated, CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF could detect AFB1 sensitively with good fluorescence recovery performance (Fig. 6C), while the bare CDs-Apt@CuS probe might not be able to resist the interference of macromolecules contained in the food samples. (Fig. 6D) In conclusion, the hollow COF embedded with fluorescence probes is suitable for practical use.

The established HCOF-protected FRET nanoprobe was applied to detect AFB1 in real food samples, including milk, beer, and dairy beverages. Compared with traditional detection method, the way to determine AFB1 based on the nanoprobe required no complicated pre-treatment steps, just a simple dilution procedure using PBS. A total of 5.0 mL liquid sample (milk and dairy beverages) were diluted with 5–20 fold, while the beer was diluted with 2–10 fold, which subsequently were used as blank sample, and the spiked samples with various concentrations of AFB1 were used as analytical samples. According to the optimized experiment conditions, the diluted sample solution was added to a test tube with a certain amount of AFB1 standard solution and the recovery test was performed. As listed in Table S4, the results showed the average recoveries were in the range of 80.9%-101.9%, confirming that the constructed CDs-Apt@CuS@HCOF nanoprobe could be sensitively applied to recognize AFB1 in real samples. Moreover, to objectively evaluate the actual interference effect of the prepared probes, we also measured the food samples without dilution. The results revealed the recoveries of spiked samples were lower than it of diluted samples. The anti-interference capability of HCOF shell is insufficient for directly analyzing the sample without any pretreatment. It is possibly due to the presence of matrix interfering substance such as abundant protein and fat, which may block the HCOF surface via non-specific adsorption and affect the interaction of target with nanoprobe. Besides, the samples without dilution probably contain a small amount of interfering substance with specific light absorption property, which would affect the fluorescence of CDs, leading to poor recovery. Then, we employed the HPLC standard method (GB 5009.22-2016) to verify the recoveries of the different diluted times. The results showed that the recoveries of the spiked AFB1 samples were close to those of the high-fold sample dilutions, possibly because the decrease in matrix concentration reduced its interference with the nanoprobe. Meanwhile, the above also demonstrated the acceptable accuracy of the constructed probes for AFB1 in practice. This analysis further indicated that the established HCOF-protective nanoprobe was suitable for effort-saving determination of AFB1 in actual food samples. Overall, the proposed method provided a novel and convenient strategy for recognition and controlling hazards in application.

In summary, this study successfully proposed a micro-capsule protection strategy coupled with rapid nanosensing platform. By removing ZIF-90 from ZIF-90@COF, the protective HCOF capsule was developed to block the macromolecular interference in the food sample application, favoring the performance of FRET-nanosensor inside. The hydrophobic surface of HCOF also contributed to the interaction of AFB1 with the nanoprobes, so that the linear response was enlarged from 1 nM to 300 nM was acquired for the AFB1 assay with a detection limit of 0.3 nM. Real sample analysis revealed that the HCOF embedded with luminescent nanoprobes achieved specific recognition towards AFB1 while common interferences hardly showed significant response. Moreover, the total recognition could be accomplished within 20 min, which satisfied the demands of routine and quick detection. This study provides new ideas for improved anti-interference and in situ inspection of hazards against complex sample matrix.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Lu Yang: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jing Wang: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Chun-Yang Li: . Man-Man Wang: . Jing-Min Liu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Shuo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272398).
